data.body:
PASS-1 PLAN REVIEW — [G6] Approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection — on the kill-switch/disable-config + rollout-ordering FOLLOW-ON plan ("T12 gap the gate found on itself").

I grounded every load-bearing premise of this plan in the shipped code (no reliance on the plan's own framing). The central conclusion: the approach is NOT fit-for-purpose because the mechanism it proposes to build ALREADY EXISTS AND SHIPS — making this a NIH/rebuild-existing approach defect rather than a sound design. Findings below are coaching, non-blocking.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] Re-ground the plan's central premise — "the gate introduces a **blocking change to the in-use `claim` path** but never specifies how to **disable/roll back the gate itself**" — against the shipped code, which contradicts it: the claim gate is an OPT-IN preference that DEFAULTS OFF, so it introduces no blocking change to the in-use claim path until a project enables it, and turning it off IS the rollback. Re-scope the ticket from "design+build a disable-config and rollback path" to "verify the existing default-off preference satisfies these ACs (and fill any genuine doc/test gap), else close as already-satisfied."
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan "Why" + "Scope" item 1 ("A project-level disable-the-gate config (gate off => claim behaves exactly as today; fail-open)") + AC line 1
  - evidence:
    - `src/rebar/config.py:260` — `require_plan_review_for_claim: bool = False` (DEFAULT OFF); the inline comment at :258-259 reads "Default off ⇒ `claim` keeps today's behavior; turning it off is the rollback (an ordinary preference, no kill-switch needed)."
    - `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:651,661-662` — `_plan_review_precheck` returns `None` (allow) when the preference is off; so when off, `claim` is unchanged.
    - The parent epic's OWN Success Criteria #4 already states this exact design: "a config PREFERENCE that turns the plan-review requirement ON/OFF ... DEFAULTS OFF ... 'rollback' is simply turning the preference off — an ordinary config preference, not a deploy-time kill-switch, and no separate rollback mechanism is needed."
  - suggested_fix: Rewrite "Why"/"Scope" to acknowledge `verify.require_plan_review_for_claim` (default False) IS the disable-the-gate config and IS the rollback; reframe AC1 from "A config flag disables the gate ... covered by a test" to "confirm the existing default-off preference + its test coverage satisfy this" (see the test note below).

- [ ] AC1's "covered by a test" clause appears already met — verify before treating it as new work. `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py:233` asserts `require_plan_review_for_claim is False` by default and :238-239 asserts the on-toggle; the gate-off path leaving `claim` at today's behavior is also an explicit edge case in the parent epic's end-to-end AC. Point AC1 at the existing test (or name the precise missing assertion) rather than implying a new test must be authored.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan AC line 1 ("... fail-open ... when off; covered by a test")
  - evidence: `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py:44,233-239` (default-off + on-toggle config behavior); parent epic end-to-end AC includes "the gate-off config preference leaving `claim` at today's behavior."

- [ ] Reconcile Scope item 2 (old+new coexistence; "older clones preserve-and-ignore the new events") and Scope item 3 (deploy ordering for the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar + signature events) with the SHIPPED rollout handling — both are already implemented and were addressed by child db7b, so this is fitness-for-purpose duplication, not a gap. State what (if anything) the shipped handling is missing, or fold these scope items into the re-scoped verification.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan "Scope" items 2 and 3 + AC line 2
  - evidence:
    - `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:14` — REVIEW_RESULT + attestation events "follow rebar's preserved-and-ignored-by-older-clones rollout (upgrade reconcile hosts first)" — i.e. both coexistence AND deploy-ordering are stated at the implementation.
    - `docs/event-schema.md:77` + `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py` — unknown event types are preserved-and-ignored, with forward-compat tests; `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:154` handles it in fsck.
    - Parent epic comment (1782271126…): "T12 (cross-clone event coexistence): db7b — REVIEW_RESULT + attestation events follow rebar's preserved-and-ignored-by-older-clones rollout (fsck WARN; reconcile hosts upgrade first)."
  - suggested_fix: Drop Scope 2/3 as net-new design and instead cross-reference `sidecar.py` + `event-schema.md` + the db7b rollout note as the existing coexistence/ordering design; if a real gap remains (e.g. the SIGNATURE head-state event vs. an old reducer), name it specifically.

- [ ] State the POSITIVE rationale tied to the ACTUAL state of the code, because as written the plan's rationale ("a gate with no off-switch is a single point of failure") rests on a premise that is false against the shipped default-off, opt-in gate. If after re-grounding a genuine residual gap survives (e.g. signing-scheme deploy ordering not yet documented in a project-facing rollback runbook), state why THAT specific gap warrants a ticket; if none survives, the positive rationale is "verify + document," which should be stated explicitly so an implementer does not build a redundant disable mechanism.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan "Why" section (whole)
  - evidence: the "Why" asserts the gate "never specifies how to disable/roll back" and "a gate with no off-switch is a single point of failure" — contradicted by `config.py:260` (default off) + `transition.py:661-662` (off ⇒ claim unchanged) + the documented "turning it off is the rollback" (`docs/plan-review-gate.md:180`, `config.py:258-259`).

APPROACH-SELECTION NOTE (G6.3): I generated the structurally-different alternative the plan missed — "treat this as a verification/doc-audit task against the already-shipped default-off preference + preserved-and-ignored rollout, not a build task." On codebase-alignment (the mechanism exists), blast-radius (zero new code vs. a redundant second disable path), simplicity, and robustness (avoids two competing kill-switch concepts), that alternative is clearly superior to "build a new disable-config + rollback path." Coaching the planner to adopt it.

AFFIRMATIONS (G6 criteria this plan's STATED goal does satisfy, once re-scoped):
- The advisory verifier's confidence-.71/critical signal correctly identifies that rollout/rollback DOCUMENTATION for the signing scheme is worth surfacing; the legitimate residual (a project-facing back-out runbook for the signature scheme) is a defensible doc deliverable even though the disable-config and event-coexistence mechanisms already exist.
- The plan correctly names the right artifacts to cross-check (the TAG_DELTA/event-rollout note, the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar) — the data-flow it points at is real.
